key_insight: A fine-tuned machine-learned potential can rapidly screen multicomponent transition metal carbides for both synthesizability and hardness, and it reveals a previously unrecognized family of stacking-ordered phases in mixed group 4/5–6 compositions.
engineering_relevance: This enables high-throughput discovery of extreme-environment carbides by narrowing the search to compositions that are both experimentally accessible and mechanically robust.
method: Fine-tuned MACE on ~28,000 DFT calculations across group 4–6 metals and carbon.
Screened 1500+ equiatomic compositions over rocksalt, hexagonal, and hcp prototypes.
Combined free-energy estimates with elasticity-based hardness surrogates to rank candidates.
evidence_signal: The model reaches about 10 meV/atom formation-energy error for thermodynamically relevant structures using only 20% of the training data, and its 1500°C synthesizability predictions align well with reported experiments.
watch_item: Free-energy and hardness screening is still an approximation, so experimentally relevant kinetics, defects, and synthesis pathways may shift which carbide phases are actually obtainable.
paper_title: Synthesizability, hardness, and stacking order in multicomponent transition metal carbides from machine-learned potentials
